coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots for discrimination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Ruiqi
Li, Yingxing
Zhu, Rongchao
Bai, Huazangnaowu
Zhao, Chuanfang
Zu, Baiyi
Cai, Zhenzhen
coredata.dc:description: It is of great significance for the discrimination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) from their analogues. Here, based on the electron-deficient properties of TNT and TNP, a series of o-phenylenediamine/polyethyleneimine carbonized polymer dots (OPD/PEI CPDs) with different densities and types of amine groups (-NH2) on the surface were designed by modulating the ratio of the precursors OPD and PEI. The surface -NH2 group of OPD/PEI CPDs could form a Meisenheimer complex with TNT, triggering the Forster resonance energy transfer, while forming hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl in TNP, triggering the charge transfer and spectral overlap to produce photoinduced electron transfer accompanied with inner filter effect. When the ratio of OPD to PEI is 1:1 and the surface -NH2 content is 26.06 %, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs demonstrated a more superior sensing performance toward targets, including a low limit of detection (TNT: 324 nM and 255 µM, TNP: 21.08 nM and 318.6 nM), a rapid response (<1 s), and a rather good selectivity in the presence of 20 interferents. Moreover, the practicality of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs was further verified by an OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor, which is capable of discriminating TNT and TNP particles and vapors as low as pg-level and ppm-level respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Explosive detection
Carbonized polymer dots
Colorimetric
Fluorescent
Forster resonance energy transfer
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the OPD/PEI CPDs for the detection of TNT and TNP
Structural characterization of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs
Discrimination mechanisms for TNT and TNP
Colorimetric discrimination of TNT and TNP
Fluorescent discrimination of TNT and TNP
OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor for TNT and TNP detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174159), the Tianshan Talents Plan (2022TSYCCX0074), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2022441), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of Xinjiang Province, the Tianshan Innovation Team Plan (2022TSYCTD0020).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174159), the Tianshan Talents Plan (2022TSYCCX0074), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2022441), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of Xinjiang Province (2024E01033), the Tianshan Innovation Team Plan (2022TSYCTD0020).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The design strategy of OPD/PEI CPDs and the regulation of the density and type of -NH2 groups on the surface of CPDs for fluorescent-colorimetric dual-mode discrimination of TNT and TNP. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of different elements of the (b) OPD/PEI1 CPDs, (c) OPD/PEI2 CPDs, (d) OPD/PEI3 CPDs. (e) The corresponding histogram of N element content for the three CPDs. Fluorescence spectra of the (f) OPD/PEI1 CPDs, (g) OPD/PEI2 CPDs, (h) OPD/PEI3 CPDs before and after detection of TNT and TNP. (i) The radar map of the major physical quantities of the three CPDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) the corresponding size distribution, (c) HRTEM image, (d) XRD pattern of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs. (e) FT-IR spectra and the (f) TGA profiles of the OPD, PEI and OPD/PEI1 CPDs.

(a) The electrostatic potential distribution diagrams of OPD/PEI1 CPDs surface characteristic structures, TNT and TNP, in which the maximal and minimal electrostatic potential surfaces as red and blue dots, respectively. (b) Scatter pattern and isosurface of the non-covalent interaction between the OPD/PEI1 CPDs surface characteristic structures and TNP. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs before and after detection of TNT and TNP, respectively. (d) The normalized emission spectrum of OPD/PEI1 CPDs and the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the Meisenheimer complex and TNP. (e) Cyclic voltammogram of OPD/PEI1 CPDs in 0.1 M Bu4NPF6-DMSO solution (50 mV s−1, N2 atmosphere). (f) Calculated electron-density distribution and the corresponding energy diagrams of the main molecular orbitals of TNT, TNP and their analogues, with positive and negative orbital phases in the red and blue regions, respectively.

(a) (i) The UV–vis absorption spectra and the corresponding optical images of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to different concentrations of TNT (0–2.0 mg/mL), and (ii) the ratio of absorption intensity at 466 nm to 376 nm (A466/A376) as a function of TNT concentration. (b) (i) The UV–vis absorption spectra and the corresponding optical images of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to different concentrations of TNP (0–50 µg/mL), and (ii) absorption intensity at 376 nm (A376) as a function of the TNP. (c) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram obtained from optical images in (a) and (b) to evaluate the color changes of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs to TNT and TNP, respectively. (d) Optical images of time-dependent colorimetric response and the corresponding RGB distances of OPD/PEI1 CPDs with the detection of TNT and TNP. (e) Absorption intensity at 376 nm changes obtained for OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to various interferents, TNT and TNP (70.4 mM).

(a) (i) The fluorescence spectra and the corresponding optical images of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to different concentrations of TNT (0–2.0 mg/mL), and (ii) the emission intensity at 553 nm (I553) as function of the TNT concentration. (b) The fluorescence spectra and the corresponding optical images of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to different concentrations of TNP (0–50 µg/mL), and (ii) the emission intensity at 553 nm (I553) as a function of TNP concentration. The time-dependent fluorescence response of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs at 553 nm for detecting (c) TNT and (d) TNP (70.4 mM). (e) Fluorescence intensity at 553 nm changes obtained for OPD/PEI1 CPDs in response to various interferents, TNT and TNP (70.4 mM). The excitation wavelength is 365 nm.

(a) Construction of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor and the fluorescence change for TNT and TNP. (b) Time-dependent images extracted from the video of the OPD/PEI1CPDs-based paper sensor during the detection of TNT particles under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Change in the corresponding diffusion diameter of the fluorescent spots as a function of time after detection of TNT on the paper sensor. Fluorescence images and the corresponding G/(R+B+G) values as a function of the concentration of (d) TNT (0–1 mM) and (e) TNP (0–1 mM) vapors, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots for discrimination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics Science and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics Science and Technology, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics Science and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Trace Chemical Substances Sensing, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Urumqi 830011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Trace Chemical Substances Sensing, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Trace Chemical Substances Sensing, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Urumqi 830011, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Physics Science and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics Science and Technology, Xinjiang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great significance for the discrimination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) from their analogues. Here, based on the electron-deficient properties of TNT and TNP, a series of o-phenylenediamine/polyethyleneimine carbonized polymer dots (OPD/PEI CPDs) with different densities and types of amine groups (-NH2) on the surface were designed by modulating the ratio of the precursors OPD and PEI. The surface -NH2 group of OPD/PEI CPDs could form a Meisenheimer complex with TNT, triggering the Forster resonance energy transfer, while forming hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl in TNP, triggering the charge transfer and spectral overlap to produce photoinduced electron transfer accompanied with inner filter effect. When the ratio of OPD to PEI is 1:1 and the surface -NH2 content is 26.06 %, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs demonstrated a more superior sensing performance toward targets, including a low limit of detection (TNT: 324 nM and 255 µM, TNP: 21.08 nM and 318.6 nM), a rapid response (<1 s), and a rather good selectivity in the presence of 20 interferents. Moreover, the practicality of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs was further verified by an OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor, which is capable of discriminating TNT and TNP particles and vapors as low as pg-level and ppm-level respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots have been increasingly employed in the fields of optics, energy and biomedicine due to their favorable biocompatibility, abundant functional groups, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness and other advantageous characteristics [1–3]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have distinctive polymer and carbon heterostructure properties, as well as a relatively low degree of carbonization, are usually synthesized through hydrothermal cross-linking carbonization utilizing small molecules or polymers as precursors [4,5]. CPDs have a unique structure with carbon core as the central and polymer chains as the shell layer, which is usually regarded as a state between fully carbonized carbon dots and polymers, with the characteristics of both quantum dots and polymers [6]. CPDs exhibit favorable characteristics of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, photoluminescence, straightforward synthesis and modification, as well as rich surface functional groups, and have been widely used in fluorescent labeling, drug delivery, bioimaging, catalysis, sensing and other fields [7–11]. The luminescence of CPDs is primarily attributable to the carbon core within them or to the interaction between specific small molecules and the polymer, which is closely related to the reaction conditions, precursors [12], etc. In addition, different precursors could affect the functional groups on the surface of the CPDs, and the adjustable abundance of the functional groups provides the possibility for a wide range of applications [13]. At present, based on the properties of the abundant surface functional groups, CPDs could be effective donors or acceptors for energy transfer [14], making them applicable for detection of the liver disease biomarkers [15], L-lysine [16], volatile organic compounds [17] in the field of biomedical sensing. However, the CPDs used for detection described above were usually synthesized in one step by considering the target feature, while the abundance of the functional groups and the efficiency of the target identification have been ignored. Therefore, whether the abundant surface functional groups on CPDs can be used to regulate the reactivity and effective collision efficiency with the target analyte through modulating the density of the recognition site, and thus to improve the detection sensitivity remains unknown, but is of great significance for the rational design of functionalized CPDs.

Nitroaromatic hydrocarbon explosives are the most widely used explosives, among which 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) are typical representatives [18]. The illegal use of TNT and TNP for terrorist attacks would pose a threat to the national security and the residues would contaminate soil and water sources, further damaging the human circulatory system and liver [19]. Therefore, the highly sensitive, rapid and on-site identification of trace TNT and TNP is of great significance for environmental and safe requirements. Traditional TNT and TNP analysis and detection methods include gas chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, X-ray imaging and metal detectors, etc., suffer from complex pretreatment and the need for well-trained technicians to operate, which limit their application in actual environments. Optical methods have been widely used in the detection of TNT and TNP due to the advantages of naked-eye recognition, immediate displaying, simple operation, etc., which comply with the requirements of on-site detection [20–22]. The materials used for TNT and TNP optical sensing mainly include fluorescent films [23,24], metal nanoparticles [25,26], organic small molecules [27,28], quantum dots [29,30], etc., with fluorescence turn-on, turn-off, or ratiometric responses. Among them, due to the electron-deficient characteristics of TNT and TNP, it is easy to realize the fluorescence turn-off response of the sensing material based on the energy transfer or electron transfer mechanisms [31–33]. At present, the fluorescence turn-off detection of TNT has been realized by constructing metal nanomaterials to generate the hydrogen bonds or Meisenheimer complex between TNT and carboxyl or amine groups, accompanying with the photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) or the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) processes [34,35]. However, most of these design strategies focus on the properties of the sensing materials towards either TNT or TNP, and there still lacks a systematic investigation on how the modulation of the structure of the sensing materials affects the discrimination of them. Therefore, whether a new functionalized CPDs design strategy with high sensitivity, rapid and trace detection towards TNT and TNP could be proposed based on the efficient employment of the recognition site remains a great challenge, but is of vital significance for developing a reliable sensing method for discrimination them from their analogues.

Herein, based on the strong electron-deficient properties of TNT and TNP, a series of o-phenylenediamine/polyethyleneimine CPDs (OPD/PEI CPDs) with electron-rich amino groups (-NH2) as the recognition site were designed and synthesized by modulating the density and type of the -NH2 groups. The surface -NH2 groups of the CPDs forms a Meisenheimer complex with TNT, triggering the FRET process as well as the fluorescence turn-off and color change, while forming hydrogen bonds with TNP, triggering the charge transfer process and the fluorescence turn-off response. It is revealed that the different surface environments of -NH2 would affect their ability to generate Meisenheimer complex with TNT and hydrogen bonds with TNP, which in turn affects the sensing performance towards the target analytes. When the ratio of OPD/PEI is 1 and the surface -NH2 content is 26.06 %, the OPD/PEI CPDs can realize the fluorescent-colorimetric dual-mode discrimination of TNT and TNP, with good sensitivity and fast response speed. The feasibility of the present CPDs design strategy was further verified by the detection of the solid, liquid, and gas phase target analytes in practical scenarios.

Unless otherwise noted, all reagents and materials were procured from commercial sources and were utilized without further purification. O-Phenylenediamine (OPD), branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw=600), KSCN, (CH3COO)2Zn, methylbenzene (MB), 1,2-dicyanobenzene (1,2-DCB), xylene isomers (DMB), ethyl 4-nitrobenzoate (4-NAE), m-dinitrobezene (m-DNB), AlCl3, NaI, o-nitrobenzoic acid (2-NA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ti(SO4)2, Gd(NO 3)3, 1,4-dimethyl-benzene (1,4-DMB) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. CaCl2, LiCl were purchased from ChengDu Chron Chemicals Co,. Ltd. 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), 2,6-dinitrotoluene (DNT) were obtained from the Ministry of State Security of China.

The morphology of OPD/PEI CPDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F2100F, JEOL) and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7610F Plus, JEOL). The zeta potential was obtained using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS9. The elemental mapping was characterized by using a scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersion detector (EDS, Oxford X-MaxN50). X-ray Diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted using powder XRD (Bruker D8 Advance). The elemental composition and content of OPD/PEI CPDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Frontier thermogravimetric infrared analysis system (PerkinElmer, Japan). A CHI 760E electro-chemical analyzer (CHI Instrument Inc., USA) was used to acquire electrochemical signals. All of the Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were measured on the Hitachi UV-3900 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and the relevant fluorescence decay profile operated in time-correlated single-photon counting. The optical microscopy images and video were performed on Nikon Ti-E inverted fluorescence microscope. Thermogravimetric analysis measurements were performed in nitrogen up to 1000 ℃ at a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1 using a Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer (STA 8000).

The quantum chemical analysis in this work is performed by the Gaussian 09 [36]. The geometric structure optimization is conducted through the density functional theory (DFT) with a M06–2X [37] and 6–31 +G(d) [38,39] basis set, and then verifies that there is no virtual frequency in each geometric structure. Subsequently, the fluorescent property is carried out by a time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) at optimized first excited state geometry with a M06–2X/ 6–31 +G(d) level. The wave function analyses, such as analysis of excited states and the quantitative analyses of electrostatic potential (ESP) [40,41] are conducted by using Multiwfn software [42]. Molecular configuration and orbital diagram is plotted by the VMD program [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the OPD/PEI CPDs for the detection of TNT and TNP
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the strong electron-deficient characteristics of TNT and TNP, a series of OPD/PEI CPDs were designed and synthesized by modulating the density and type of -NH2 groups on the surface of the CPDs (Fig. S1). Since the -NH2 groups on the surface of OPD/PEI CPDs and TNT with strong electrophilicity are prone to formation of Meisenheimer complex, which in turn promotes the generation of FRET process from OPD/PEI CPDs to Meisenheimer complex, thus enabling the detection of TNT ( Fig. 1a). While based on the hydrogen bonding force between the -NH2 groups and the -OH groups in TNP, the PET process from OPD/PEI CPDs to TNP is triggered, so as to realize the detection of TNP with different sensing performance from TNT. To investigate the effect of different surface -NH2 groups environments on the ability to generate Meisenheimer complex with TNT and hydrogen bonds with TNP, which in turn affects the sensing performance towards the target analytes, the ratio of OPD to PEI was regulated during the synthesis process. From the SEM images, the three OPD/PEI CPDs are spherical, and their diameters increase from 3.74 nm to 370 nm as the mass ratio of OPD to PEI increases from 1:1–3:1, indicating that OPD is conducive to increasing the cross-linking density of CPDs and further improving its size (Fig. S2). According to the zeta potential analysis, the zeta potential values are all positive due to the abundance of -NH2 groups on the surface of OPD/PEI CPDs, and with the increase of OPD content, the value enhanced from + 21.51 mV to + 28.25 mV, indicating that its dispersion stability is improved, which is conducive to practical application (Fig. S3). According to the EDS mapping images, when the mass ratio of OPD to PEI is 1:1, the characteristic elements C, N and O, which belongs to the CPDs, are homogeneous distributed in the entire observed area in the form of clusters, and with the increase of PEI content, there is obvious aggregation due to the enhancement of hydrogen bonding force (Figs. 1b-1d). Further comparison of the characteristic element N content in the three CPDs shows that with the increase of PEI content, the N content increased from 21.63 % to 26.06 %, which is consistent with the change trend of N content in OPD and PEI before polymerization (Fig. 1e). The XPS of OPD/PEI CPDs further confirms the presence of C, N and O elements, and the N content is between 17.92 % and 21.96 %, the C 1 s and N 1 s analysis show that OPD/PEI CPDs contain C-N, CC and C-C bonds, which is consistent with the theoretical structure, indicating that the CPDs are successfully prepared (Fig. S4-S6). From the fluorescence spectra, the three OPD/PEI CPDs have the emission peaks at 553 nm, 561 nm and 562 nm, and when the addition of TNT, the fluorescence intensity decreases by 44.88 %, 42.55 %, 43.74 % respectively, accompanied by the red-shift of 11 nm, 8 nm and 9 nm (Figs. 1f-1h). When detecting TNP, the fluorescence intensity of the three OPD/PEI CPDs decreased by 57.51 %, 64.46 %, 56.97 %, but new shoulders appeared at 507 nm, 511 nm, 511 nm respectively, and the quenching efficiency enhanced with the increase of PEI content (Fig. S7, S8). Subsequently, by comparing the surface -NH2 density and luminescence intensity of the three OPD/PEI CPDs, as well as the quenching efficiency and the red-shift degree before and after the detection of TNT and TNP, it can be seen that with the increase of surface -NH2 groups content, the better sensing performance of CPDs towards TNT and TNP (Fig. 1i, Table S1). Through the above analysis, modulating the -NH2 density on the surface of the CPDs can improve its sensing performance for TNT and TNP, and OPD/PEI1 CPD is confirmed to be the most efficient one among these regulated CPDs, which further proves the feasibility of the proposed CPDs design strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and compositions of OPD/PEI1 CPDs were systematically analyzed to verify their effect on the detection performance towards TNT and TNP. According to the TEM image, a large amount of well-monodisperse spherical OPD/PEI1 CPDs with a diameter of about several nanometers could be obviously observed in the whole area ( Fig. 2a). By statistically counting 100 polymer particles, the average diameter of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs could be determined to be around 3.74 nm, which means that it has a certain size limiting effect (Fig. 2b). From the HRTEM image, the carbon core of OPD/PEI1 CPDs exhibits a distinct lattice fringe with a plane spacing of 0.194 nm, which is different from the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm for graphite or graphene quantum dots (Fig. 2c). This deviation from the characteristic lattice of graphite may be attributed to either a regular arrangement of the polymer chains or the presence of additional, slight carbonation crystallization. Furthermore, the crystal region is recognizable and surrounded by an amorphous region, indicating that the carbon core may be encapsulated by a substantial number of PEI. In the XRD images, there is only a broad peak at approximately 21.35° could be discerned, which belongs to the characteristic of CPDs with polymer shell and highly disordered carbon (Fig. 2d). The FT-IR showed that the N-H and CC stretching vibration peaks appeared at 3420 cm−1 and 1628 cm−1 respectively, which could be attributed to the formation of carbon core by dehydration carbonization, indicating the OPD/PEI1 CPDs rich in -NH2 on the surface was successfully synthesized (Fig. 2e). In addition, it can be seen from the TGA profiles that when the temperature is 206 °C, the OPDs are completely thermally decomposed, while the weight loss of PEI is only 2.41 %, which is negligible, and the OPD/PEI1 CPDs is 12.08 %, which can be fully attributed to the uncarbonized OPD (Fig. 2f). When the temperature rises to 403 °C, the PEI completely decomposes, and the weight loss of OPD/PEI1 CPDs is 80 %, indicating that the uncarbonized PEI content on its surface is 67.92 %. Further calculations showed that the molar ratio of aliphatic amines and aromatic amines on the surface of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs is 17:3, indicating that aliphatic amines were more likely to interact with TNT and TNP to produce spectral responses. Furthermore, the absorption and fluorescence emission intensity of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs remained unchanged with the increase of time under room temperature, incandescent lamp and 365 nm UV light lamp irradiation, indicating that the OPD/PEI1 CPDs exhibited significant long-term storage stability (Fig. S9-S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination mechanisms for TNT and TNP
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the reactivity of OPD/PEI1 CPDs to TNT and TNP, the electrostatic potential (ESP) of the polymer chain active units on the designed OPD/PEI1 CPDs surface and analytes were investigated ( Fig. 3a). The ESP values of the polymer chain active units on the OPD/PEI1 CPDs surface are negative near the amino groups, and the PEI segment is 27.97 kcal/mol lower than that of OPDs, indicating that the aliphatic amino groups are more likely to attack the electron-deficient group. Similarly, the ESP values of the TNT and TNP are positive near the benzene ring, indicating that they are electrophilic and tend to be close to the amino groups on the surface of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs. By analyzing the ESPs of the precursors PEI and OPD and their binding energies to TNT, it can be seen that TNT is more easily bind to PEI which containing aliphatic primary amines, thereby increasing the probability of generating the Mersenheimer complex (Fig. S12). In addition, the interaction between the polymer chain active units on the OPD/PEI1 CPDs surface and TNP was investigated by the independent gradient model (IGM) (Fig. 3b). There are strong hydrogen bonds between the -OH in TNP and the -NH2, -NH- in the OPD and PEI segments respectively, which provide a prerequisite for the occurrence of the PET process from OPD/PEI1 CPDs to TNP. The strong attraction between TNP and the OPD segment is verified by discrete points in the scatter graph of similar patterns with spikes around the negative sign(λ2)ρ(−0.02) and high δg (>0.04), which is 1.26 times higher than of the PEI segment, indicating that TNP is more likely to form strong hydrogen bonds with aromatic amines. The above results further verify that the increase of PEI content could improve the affinity of OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNT and TNP, and further improve the detection performance, which was consistent with the experimental results.

According to analysis the fluorescence decay curves, the average fluorescence lifetime of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs decreased from 6.9689 ns to 6.9588 ns after the addition of TNT, which belonged to the dynamic fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3c, Table S2). When detecting TNP, the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 6.9689 ns to 6.4069 ns, with 0.5519 ns lower than that of detecting TNT, which can also be attributed to the dynamic fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the emission spectrum of OPD/PEI1 CPDs overlapped obviously with the absorption spectrum of the Meisenheimer complex formed by TNT, and combined with dynamic fluorescence quenching, the sensing mechanism of OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNT could be attributed to FRET (Fig. 3d). For the detection of TNP, the absorption peak of TNP itself at 352 nm partially overlapped with the emission spectrum of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs, which can be attributed to the fluorescence quenching mechanism based on the inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. S13). To further verify the sensing mechanism of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs to TNP, the electrochemical oxidation and reduction potentials of OPD/PEI1 CPDs were determined by cyclic voltammetry, the optical energy gap of OPD/PEI1 CPDs were estimated by the Tauc plot (Fig. 3e, Fig. S14, Fig. S15). The highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs were calculated as EHOMO = -4.89 eV and ELUMO = -1.84 eV respectively, and the optical energy gap was calculated to be 3.05 eV (Equation S2, S3). Furthermore, the LUMO of TNP was calculated to be −3.38 eV according to the density functional theory (DFT), which was lower than that of OPD/PEI1 CPDs, indicating that the electrons in the excited state of OPD/PEI1 CPDs could be transferred to TNP (Fig. 3f). The LUMO energy level of TNP is 0.19 eV-3.60 eV lower than their analogues, guaranteeing spontaneous transfer of electrons. Considering the spectral overlap, dynamic quenching and energy level matching, the sensing mechanism of OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNP could be attributed to PET accompanied with IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric discrimination of TNT and TNP
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the colorimetric detection performance of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNT and TNP after optimizing the pH condition with a value of 8, a series of TNT and TNP solutions with concentrations of 0–2.0 mg/mL and 0–50 µg/mL were prepared, respectively (Fig. S16, S17). When the concentration of TNT is 0.2 mg/mL, a new absorption peak appears at 376 nm, and its intensity increases rapidly, reaching stability at a concentration of 1 mg/mL (Fig 4ai). At the same time, when the TNT concentration reaches 0.4 mg/mL, a distinct absorption centered at 466 nm is observed, which gradually intensifies with the increase of TNT concentration, accompanied by the color changed from colorless to reddish-brown and gradually deepened. Furthermore, the absorption intensity ratio (A466/A376) exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of TNT within the range of 0–1 mg/mL (Fig 4aii). The limit of detection (LOD) for OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNT was determined to be 255 µM using the 3σ/k method, where σ represents the standard deviation of the absorption intensity ratio of OPD/PEI1 CPDs solution at 466 nm and 376 nm after conducting 11 tests. For the detection of TNP, with the increase of TNP concentration, the absorption peak at 376 nm gradually increased, and the color changed from colorless to yellow-green (Fig 4bi). The absorption intensity exhibited a linear relationship with the TNP concentration in the range of 0–25 µg/mL, and the LOD was calculated to be 318.6 nM with a σ of 0.0012, which was 800 times lower than that of TNT, indicating that the surface -NH2 groups of OPD/PEI1 CPDs was more prone to trigger the PET process by forming hydrogen bonds (Fig 4bii). According to the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram obtained from the optical images of OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards different concentrations of TNT and TNP, the color changes linearly in two different directions as the analyte concentration increases, indicating that TNT and TNP can be discrimination in the colorimetric channel ( Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the absorption intensity of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNT and TNP were almost unchanged with the increase of temperature to 120 ℃, indicating that the OPD/PEI1 CPDs has excellent environmental stability (Fig. S18, S19).

The time-dependent colorimetric images were recorded to evaluate the response time and stability of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNT and TNP (Fig. 4d).The characteristic colorimetric signal response can be observed within 0.25 s for both TNT and TNP, and the average RGB distance extracted from the optical images shows that the colorimetric signal tends to stabilize at 4 s and 2 s, respectively. To investigate the selectivity of OPD/PEI1 CPDs towards TNT and TNP, 20 kinds of interferents consisting of nitroaromatic compounds, common anions and cations, as well as starches and flours, which are common in daily life, were selected (Fig. 4e). After adding various interferents (70.4 mM), the absorption spectrum of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs did not change significantly, while the characteristic absorption peaks appeared after the addition of 70.4 mM TNT and TNP (Fig. S20). It should be noted that their structural analogues, including DNT, m-DNB, 4-NAE, cannot interact with OPD/PEI1 CPDs to trigger energy or electron transfer processes, indicating that the designed OPD/PEI1 CPDs has unique selectivity for TNT and TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent discrimination of TNT and TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensitivity of OPD/PEI1 CPDs at pH 8 for TNT and TNP was investigated in the concentration range of 0–2 mg/mL and 0–500 µg/mL, respectively (Fig. S21, S22). As the concentration of TNT increased to 2.0 mg/mL, the emission peak at 553 nm gradually shifted to 590 nm, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, accompanied by the gradual quenching of yellow fluorescence (Fig. 5ai). The fluorescence intensity (I553) showed a linear relationship with the TNT concentration in the range of 0–2 mg/mL, and the LOD of OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNT was calculated to be 324 nM with a σ of 2.7 (Fig. 5aii). For the detection of TNP, with the increase of TNP concentration, the emission peaks at 449 nm and 553 nm gradually weakened, and the fluorescence intensity at 553 nm decreased 2.3 times faster than that at 449 nm, accompanied by the fluorescence gradually changed from yellow to colorless (Fig. 5bi). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity (I553) was linearly proportional to the TNP concentration in the range of 0–80 µg/mL, and the LOD of OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNP was determined as 21.08 nM (Fig. 5bii). Even when the temperature rises to 120 ℃, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs maintains stable fluorescence sensing performance (Fig. S23, S24).

The time-dependent fluorescence intensity observed at 553 nm was recorded to evaluate the response time and stability to TNT, TNP. The fluorescence response trend of OPD/PEI1 CPDs toward TNT and TNP at 553 nm was similar, and its fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly at first, and then tended to stabilize after 10 s ( Figs. 5c, 5d). Furthermore, various interferents did not produce significant fluorescence responses to OPD/PEI1 CPDs, indicating the desirable specificity of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNT and TNP (Fig. 5e, Fig. S25). From the above results, the LODs of OPD/PEI1 CPDs for TNT and TNP in fluorescent mode are 787 and 15 times lower than those in colorimetric mode respectively, which confirms the superior sensitivity of fluorescent mode with equally good specificity. Furthermore, OPD/PEI1 CPDs has obvious advantages over other reported materials, including colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode discrimination of TNT and TNP, and lower detection limits (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor for TNT and TNP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further enhance the capability of OPD/PEI1 CPDs to discriminate TNT, TNP solid particles and vapors on-site, an OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor was prepared by attaching OPD/PEI1 CPDs to commercial filter paper ( Fig. 6a). SEM shows that the paper has a fibrous structure, and when the probe is loaded onto the paper, solid particles with a size between 0.4 and 4.80 µm are scattered on the surface of the fibrous structure, which is agglomerated OPD/PEI1 CPDs particles (Fig. S26). Furthermore, after loading OPD/PEI1 CPDs, the fluorescence of the paper at 540 nm was enhanced, and there was a redshift of 7 nm compared with the emission peak of OPD/PEI1 CPDs, which may be caused by the hydrogen bonding force between the -NH2 on the surface of OPD/PEI1 CPDs and the -OH on the surface of paper, which verified the successful preparation of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor (Fig. S27-S28). According to the real-time detection images for different masses of TNT particles extracted from dynamic videos, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor exhibits brown fluorescence emission upon contact with the TNT particles regardless of the size, and the fluorescent area increases with time due to the dissolution and diffusion of the TNT particles (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, the fluorescence response of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor towards different masses of TNP particles is similar to that of TNT, with a dark green fluorescence emission (Fig. S29). It is notable that the fluorescence signals exhibited an increase in brightness with the increase in mass of the TNT and TNP particles, and the mass of individual TNT and TNP particles is estimated based on the approximate spherical size of the initial fluorescence emission, with the corresponding time defined as 0 s. The diffusion area counted by pixels versus time reveals that the TNT particle with a mass of 17.80 pg diffuses from 0.43 to 381.3 µm2 in 80 s, the diffusion ratio is 20 times higher than that of the TNT particle with a mass of 480.6 pg (Fig. 6c). The diffusion ratio of TNP particles with a mass of 18.99 pg within 20 s is 10 times higher than that of a mass of 854.55 pg, indicating that the smaller the mass of TNT and TNP particles, the easier it is to dissolve and diffuse, and the weaker the fluorescence signal (Fig. S30). To further evaluate the reliability of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor in sensing TNT and TNP residues in complex environment, test samples were prepared with different masses of TNT/TNP in mixing with potting soil, respectively. When in contact with the test samples, the paper sensor can immediately produce the same fluorescence changes as the TNT and TNP alone (Fig. S31, S32). In addition, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor also has a relatively obvious response to pg-level TNT and TNP residues, which indicates that the paper sensor is highly promising for ultratrace TNT and TNP solid residue tracking and discrimination.

Furthermore, for vapor detection, the reddish-brown emission belonging to the Meisenheimer complex occurs when the TNT vapor concentration was 0.293 ppm, and the yellow-green fluorescence of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor gradually fades and the reddish brown gradually brightens as the TNT vapor concentration increases (Fig. 6d). In contrast, the yellow-green fluorescence of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor gradually quenches as the TNP vapor concentration increases to 0.334 ppm, when the TNP concentration is 2.919 ppm, the yellow fluorescence appears and gradually brightens as the concentration increases (Fig. 6e). Therefore, the constructed OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensors, which exhibit high sensitivity and a rapid response, have the potential to facilitate the discrimination of ultra-trace TNT and TNP particles and vapors.

In summary, we demonstrated that the CPDs’ different surface environments of -NH2 is of great significance for regulating the sensing performance towards electron-deficient analytes. Based on the strong electron-deficient properties of TNT and TNP, three OPD/PEI CPDs with different densities and types of -NH2 groups on the surface, including aliphatic amines and aromatic amines, were prepared by modulating the mass ratio of the precursors (OPD/PEI are 1, 2, 3). It was shown that the increase of the content of the aliphatic -NH2 groups belonging to PEI on the surface of OPD/PEI CPDs could improve the affinity with TNT and TNP, thereby improving the detection performance. When the ratio of OPD/PEI is 1 and the surface -NH2 content is 26.06 %, the OPD/PEI1 CPDs can realize the fluorescent-colorimetric dual-mode discrimination of TNT and TNP, with good fluorescent and colorimetric detection limits (TNT: 324 nM and 255 µM, TNP: 21.08 nM and 318.6 nM), rapid response (<1 s), and excellent specificity even in the presence of 20 kinds of interferents. Furthermore, this material design has been proven to be reliable to discriminate trace TNT and TNP particles and vapors through the construction of the OPD/PEI1 CPDs-based paper sensor. We expect that the present OPD/PEI CPDs design strategy could provide new ideas for the discrimination of trace analytes with similar structures or properties, and be applied in many fields such as environmental monitoring, homeland security, etc. In addition, the colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing materials designed in this work on the one hand could be combined with other sensing technologies such as Raman and electrochemistry to improve the detection accuracy towards target analytes in complex environments, and on the other hand, convolutional neural networks could be constructed through machine learning to analyze small differences in visual images and improve the ability to distinguish and identify similar signals.

TNT and TNP are typical representatives of explosives, and their long-term accumulation can lead to pollution of terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems, adversely affecting the environment and human health. Furthermore, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency identified TNP and TNT as potential carcinogens, warning that excessive exposure could damage organs. Thus, the identification of trace TNT and TNP is of great significance for environmental and safety requirements. This work proposed an effective modulation strategy by regulating the surface environments of -NH2 of OPD/PEI1 CPDs to realize ultrasensitive (pg-level), rapid response and discrimination of TNT and TNP from analogues in colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode.

Li Yingxing: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qiao Ruiqi: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Cai Zhenzhen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhao Chuanfang: Software, Formal analysis. Zu Baiyi: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Zhu Rongchao: Software, Formal analysis. Bai Huazangnaowu: Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
There are no authors other than those listed in the article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174159), the Tianshan Talents Plan (2022TSYCCX0074), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2022441), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of Xinjiang Province (2024E01033), the Tianshan Innovation Team Plan (2022TSYCTD0020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2025.137944.
